title: Linux Process Management
description: Master process inspection, signaling, priority control, background jobs, and persistent execution with nohup and job control.

tasks.prompt: Display running processes in a tree format showing parent-child relationships.
tasks.hint: Use ps with aux and forest format: 'ps auxf'
tasks.explanation: 'ps auxf' lists all processes with the forest ('f') option drawing an ASCII tree of process hierarchy.

tasks.prompt: Find the PIDs of all processes whose name matches 'nginx'.
tasks.hint: Use pgrep with the process name: 'pgrep nginx'
tasks.explanation: 'pgrep' searches the process table and returns PIDs matching the given name pattern.

tasks.prompt: Send SIGTERM to all processes named 'stale_worker'.
tasks.hint: Use pkill with the process name: 'pkill stale_worker'
tasks.explanation: 'pkill' sends the default SIGTERM signal to processes matching the specified name.

tasks.prompt: Send SIGTERM (signal 15) to process with PID 5678 for graceful shutdown.
tasks.explanation: SIGTERM (15) requests a process to terminate gracefully, allowing cleanup before exit.

tasks.prompt: Forcefully kill process with PID 9999 using SIGKILL.
tasks.explanation: SIGKILL (-9) cannot be caught or ignored; the kernel immediately terminates the process.

tasks.prompt: Start 'backup.sh' with a lower priority (nice value 10).
tasks.explanation: 'nice' launches a command with adjusted scheduling priority. Higher nice values mean lower CPU priority.

tasks.prompt: Change the nice value of PID 4321 to 5.
tasks.hint: Use renice with priority and PID: 'renice 5 -p 4321'
tasks.explanation: 'renice' adjusts the priority of a running process identified by its PID.

tasks.prompt: List all background jobs in the current shell session.
tasks.explanation: 'jobs' displays background and suspended tasks started from the current shell.

tasks.prompt: Bring background job number 1 to the foreground.
tasks.explanation: 'fg %N' resumes job N in the foreground, reconnecting it to the terminal.

tasks.prompt: Run 'long_task.sh' immune to hangups, redirecting output to nohup.out by default.
tasks.explanation: 'nohup' runs a command that continues after the terminal closes, ignoring SIGHUP signals.
